figures.caption: Fig. 1. The PINN training cycle. After a one-time initialization (dashed box), the network iterates through four stages: a dual forward pass computing both the prediction ŷ and its derivative ŷ′, evaluation of the composite loss, backpropagation of parametric gradients — where the product rule arising from the time derivative ŷ′ enters in hidden layers — and a gradient-based parameter update. This cycle repeats until the total loss converges to a prescribed tolerance.

Fig. 5. Validation of the trained PINN against the analytical solution y(t) = e−t. (a) PINN prediction ŷ(t) (dashed line with circle markers) overlaid on the exact solution (solid line); the curves are visually indistinguishable. (b) Pointwise absolute error |ŷ(t)− y(t)| on a logarithmic scale. The maximum error of 5.038× 10−4 occurs at t ≈ 0.26.

Fig. 2. Architecture of the 1-3-3-1 MLP. The number of trainable parameters for a dense layer is (win×wout)+wout, where win is the layer input dimension and wout is the number of its neurons. For example, the transition between the two hidden layers (both with 3 neurons) has (3×3)+3 = 12 parameters.
Table 16 PINN predictions and absolute errors at selected points.

Fig. 4. Evolution of the total loss L and its components (LR and λ · LIC) during 15,000 training epochs, displayed on a logarithmic scale. The total loss decreases by approximately 6.3 orders of magnitude. After the initial phase, LR dominates the optimization.

Fig. 3. Detailed operation of a single artificial neuron. The inputs are linearly combined using weights and biases to form z, which is then passed through the non-linear activation function ϕ(z) to produce the output a.
